evidence_quotes.quote: Video games are not just entertainment; they are cultural artifacts, creative works, and increasingly, historical records of modern art and design.

paragraphs.text: I strongly urge the Commission to include video games within the scope of digital fairness and preservation as this industry has become quite anti consumer & laws are very behind on it. Video games are not just entertainment; they are cultural artifacts, creative works, and increasingly, historical records of modern art and design. Yet they remain one of the most fragile digital mediums, often being lost due to DRM, delistings, server shutdowns, or anti-piracy restrictions. A growing number of games are no longer accessible in any form In the multiple Hundreds,. These are titles that people purchased with real money, only to see them vanish with no legal way to retrieve or preserve them. racking up hundreds of million of Euros into the void collectively. When one person loses access to a game they paid 60 for, that is not just a digital hiccup, it is a breach of trust. Multiply that by millions of users across Europe, and it becomes systemic erasure of value. We would never accept this with music or film. Imagine Netflix deleting movies you bought because a license expired. Yet in gaming, this practice is normalized for multiple decades. I believe this issue fits perfectly alongside the goals of the Digital Fairness Act, which aims to ensure transparency, consumer rights, and access in the digital marketplace. If the Act wants to fairly represent digital ownership and protect European consumers, it must recognize that games are being excluded from the same standards applied to other media. Europe has a real chance to lead in protecting digital culture. Without clear legal safeguards, we risk losing entire generations of interactive media, not to piracy, but to silence.
